stem.rawText: В‘ВҷВҸВғВҗВӣВ”ВҮВғВҺВ”В‘В‘В–В•ВҶВ‘ВҮВ•В–ВҠВҮВҮВ“В—ВғВ–ВӢВ‘Вҗ3вҖ« Э”вҖ¬а¬№ аөҶ 10вҖ« Э”вҖ¬а¬· аөҶ 120вҖ« Э”вҖ¬аө… 30 аөҢ 0ВҠВғВҳВҮЗ«

solution.rawText:
Commentary:          This is not something that you will have learnt how to do, and in
      general, this is a very difficult problem indeed. But we can use the tools at our disposal
      to do something helpful. We donвҖҷt know how to solve a quintic (x5 ) equation, but we can
      at least try to find the turning (stationary) points, and see where that gets us.

We attempt to sketch the graph of y = 3x5 вҲ’ 10x3 вҲ’ 120x + 30, or at least think about what a
sketch might look like.
We calculate
               dy
                  = 15x4 вҲ’ 30x2 вҲ’ 120 = 15(x4 вҲ’ 2x2 вҲ’ 8) = 15(x2 вҲ’ 4)(x2 + 2)
               dx

so there are stationary points when dx
                                    dy
                                       = 0, that is, when x = 2 and when x = вҲ’2. We can now
calculate the y-coordinates: when x = вҲ’2, y = вҲ’36 + 80 + 240 + 30 > 0 (we do not need to
calculate the exact value), and when x = 2, y = 36 вҲ’ 80 вҲ’ 240 + 30 < 0.
So the graph must look something like the following:

                                                y



                                (вҲ’2, y)




                                                                             x




                                                         (2, y)



There are clearly 3 points of intersection of this graph with y = 0, so the original equation has
3 real roots, and the answer is C.
